title: Reproductive hormones — menstrual cycle and fertility
examTip: The menstrual cycle is one of the more complex B5 topics because there are four hormones interacting in sequence. AQA will ask you to sequence them through the cycle, explain contraception, and explain how IVF works. Don't try to memorise it as a list — understand the story and the sequence will follow.
introTips: You're going to learn how four hormones coordinate a 28-day cycle — and how doctors can manipulate those hormones to prevent or enable pregnancy.
The four hormones are FSH, oestrogen, LH, and progesterone. Learn what each does in sequence, and the cycle will make sense rather than just being a list.
The applications — contraception and IVF — follow directly from understanding the hormones. Once you understand what FSH does, you'll immediately see why it's used in fertility treatment.
intro: Your menstrual cycle is one of the most precisely timed biological systems in the human body. Over 28 days, four hormones work in sequence — each triggering or suppressing the next — to prepare the uterus for pregnancy and release a single egg at exactly the right moment. Understanding this cycle doesn't just explain reproductive biology; it explains how the contraceptive pill prevents pregnancy and how IVF treatments help people who can't conceive naturally. All of that comes from knowing what each hormone does and when.

keyPoints.heading: The four hormones and what they do
keyPoints.content: Four hormones control the menstrual cycle, produced by two structures: the pituitary gland and the ovaries. FSH (follicle-stimulating hormone) is secreted by the pituitary gland. It stimulates the development of a follicle (an egg in its surrounding cells) in the ovary, and it triggers the ovary to produce oestrogen. Oestrogen is produced by the ovaries. It causes the uterus lining to thicken (repair and rebuild after menstruation), inhibits further FSH production, and at high concentrations triggers a surge of LH from the pituitary. LH (luteinising hormone) is secreted by the pituitary gland. The LH surge causes ovulation — the release of the egg from the follicle (around day 14). Progesterone is produced by the corpus luteum (the remains of the follicle after the egg is released). It maintains the uterus lining during the second half of the cycle, ready for a fertilised egg to implant. It also inhibits FSH and LH. If no fertilisation occurs, progesterone levels fall, the uterus lining breaks down, and menstruation begins — restarting the cycle.
writeBullets: FSH (pituitary): stimulates follicle development and oestrogen production
Oestrogen (ovaries): thickens uterus lining; inhibits FSH; triggers LH surge
LH (pituitary): causes ovulation (egg release from follicle) — day ~14
Progesterone (corpus luteum): maintains uterus lining; inhibits FSH and LH
No fertilisation → progesterone falls → lining breaks down → menstruation → cycle restarts

keyTerms.def: Secreted by the pituitary gland; stimulates follicle development and oestrogen production
Secreted by the ovaries; thickens uterus lining, inhibits FSH, triggers LH surge
Secreted by the pituitary gland; triggers ovulation
Secreted by the corpus luteum; maintains uterus lining and inhibits FSH/LH
The release of an egg from the ovary — triggered by the LH surge, approximately day 14
The structure that remains after ovulation; secretes progesterone to maintain the uterus lining

keyPoints.memTip: FOLP — FSH, Oestrogen, LH, Progesterone. Think of the cycle as a relay race: FSH passes the baton to oestrogen; oestrogen passes it to LH; LH triggers ovulation; progesterone holds everything ready. If fertilisation doesn't happen, the relay resets.

keyPoints.heading: Negative feedback in the menstrual cycle
keyPoints.content: The menstrual cycle is regulated by negative feedback loops between the pituitary gland and the ovaries. Oestrogen inhibits FSH — as oestrogen levels rise (triggered by FSH), FSH production falls. This prevents too many follicles developing at once. At very high concentrations, oestrogen switches from inhibiting to stimulating — triggering the LH surge that causes ovulation. After ovulation, progesterone inhibits both FSH and LH — preventing another follicle from developing and another egg being released during the same cycle. When progesterone levels fall (if no fertilisation), the inhibition lifts, FSH rises, and a new cycle begins. These feedback loops create the precise 28-day rhythm.
writeBullets: Oestrogen inhibits FSH → prevents multiple follicles developing
High oestrogen → triggers LH surge → ovulation
Progesterone inhibits FSH and LH → prevents another follicle developing mid-cycle
Progesterone falls → inhibition lifted → FSH rises → new cycle begins
Negative feedback maintains the 28-day rhythm

keyTerms.def: A mechanism where rising hormone levels inhibit their own production or their stimulating hormone, maintaining cycle timing

keyPoints.memTip: Oestrogen inhibits FSH (but at peak, triggers LH surge — this is the exception). Progesterone inhibits both FSH and LH. Write: 'Oestrogen suppresses FSH. Progesterone suppresses FSH and LH.' These two inhibition lines are the most commonly lost marks in menstrual cycle questions.

keyPoints.heading: Contraception — how the pill prevents pregnancy
keyPoints.content: The oral contraceptive pill uses synthetic oestrogen and progesterone to prevent pregnancy. Oestrogen and progesterone at consistently high levels mimic the hormonal state of pregnancy — the pituitary 'thinks' the body is already pregnant and does not release FSH. Without FSH, no follicle develops and no egg matures. Without a mature egg, pregnancy is impossible even if sex occurs. Some pills also work by thickening cervical mucus, making it harder for sperm to reach an egg, and preventing the uterus lining from thickening fully. The pill is highly effective when taken correctly. It contains hormones, so it does not protect against STIs.
writeBullets: Oral contraceptive pill contains synthetic oestrogen and/or progesterone
High oestrogen/progesterone inhibits FSH release from the pituitary
No FSH → no follicle development → no egg matures → no ovulation
Without a mature egg, pregnancy cannot occur
Pill does NOT protect against STIs

keyTerms.def: A contraceptive containing synthetic oestrogen and/or progesterone that prevents ovulation by inhibiting FSH
Artificially produced hormones with the same chemical effect as natural hormones — used in contraception and fertility treatment

keyPoints.memTip: The pill uses oestrogen to block FSH. No FSH = no egg. The mechanism is the same as the natural feedback loop — high oestrogen suppresses FSH. The pill just keeps oestrogen high artificially to switch FSH off permanently.

keyPoints.heading: Fertility treatment — IVF and hormone stimulation
keyPoints.content: For people who cannot conceive naturally — often because they don't ovulate regularly or the sperm cannot reach the egg — fertility treatments use hormones to assist. IVF (in vitro fertilisation) involves stimulating the ovaries to produce multiple eggs, fertilising them outside the body, and then implanting the embryo(s) into the uterus. The hormonal process: the woman is given FSH and LH injections to stimulate the development and maturation of multiple follicles (superovulation). The mature eggs are collected, fertilised with sperm in a laboratory, and the resulting embryos are allowed to develop for a few days. One or two embryos are then selected and implanted into the uterus. Progesterone may be given to maintain the uterus lining and improve implantation chances. IVF success rates vary and multiple attempts may be needed.
writeBullets: IVF = in vitro fertilisation — fertilisation outside the body
FSH and LH injections given to stimulate multiple egg production (superovulation)
Eggs collected, fertilised with sperm in laboratory
Embryos develop for a few days, then one or two implanted into uterus
Progesterone may be given to maintain uterus lining for implantation
IVF success is not guaranteed — multiple attempts often needed

keyTerms.def: A fertility treatment where eggs are fertilised by sperm outside the body, then implanted into the uterus
The stimulation of the ovaries to produce multiple eggs using FSH/LH injections — used in IVF
The attachment of a fertilised embryo to the uterus lining — the step after IVF embryo transfer

keyPoints.memTip: IVF sequence: inject FSH/LH → collect eggs → fertilise in lab → grow embryos → implant. Five steps. Write them as a numbered list from memory. The exam often asks you to 'describe how IVF is carried out' — this list is the answer.

tables.title: The four hormones of the menstrual cycle
rows: Stimulates follicle development; stimulates oestrogen production
Thickens uterus lining; triggers LH surge at high levels

0.question: Which hormone triggers ovulation?
0.correctFeedback: ✅ Correct — the LH surge (caused by high oestrogen levels) triggers ovulation at around day 14 of the cycle.
0.wrongFeedback: Ovulation is triggered by LH (luteinising hormone). FSH stimulates the follicle and oestrogen production. Oestrogen triggers the LH surge (by stimulating the pituitary at high concentrations). Progesterone maintains the uterus lining after ovulation.

1.question: How does the contraceptive pill prevent pregnancy?
options: It kills sperm before they can reach an egg
It contains synthetic oestrogen and progesterone that inhibit FSH, preventing follicle development and ovulation
It prevents the uterus from forming at all
It contains FSH that stimulates too many eggs, most of which are infertile
1.correctFeedback: ✅ Exactly right — the pill suppresses FSH using synthetic oestrogen/progesterone. No FSH means no follicle development and no egg. Without an egg, pregnancy cannot occur.
1.wrongFeedback: The pill does not kill sperm. It works by maintaining high levels of synthetic oestrogen and/or progesterone, which inhibit FSH release from the pituitary gland. No FSH means no follicle develops and no egg matures — so there is nothing to fertilise.

3.question: In IVF treatment, why are FSH and LH injections given to the woman?
options: To prevent ovulation until the right moment
To stimulate the ovaries to produce multiple eggs for collection
To thicken the uterus lining before egg collection
To replace the hormones the ovaries cannot produce
3.correctFeedback: ✅ Right — FSH and LH injections stimulate superovulation, causing multiple follicles to develop and mature so that more than one egg can be collected. More eggs = more chances of successful fertilisation.
3.wrongFeedback: FSH and LH injections are given to stimulate superovulation — the production of multiple mature eggs. This gives doctors more eggs to work with, increasing the chances of at least one fertilising successfully and developing into a viable embryo.

commonMistakes: Confusing which hormone does what — the most common mix-up is saying FSH triggers ovulation. It doesn't. LH triggers ovulation. FSH stimulates the follicle and oestrogen production. Drill the hormone-action pairs separately.

Forgetting that oestrogen inhibits FSH — this is a key piece of the feedback loop. Many students know what oestrogen does to the uterus lining but forget its feedback role. AQA regularly tests both functions.

Saying the contraceptive pill 'stops ovulation' without explaining why — the full answer is: pill contains synthetic oestrogen/progesterone → inhibits FSH → no follicle development → no egg → no ovulation. One-word answers lose marks.

Not explaining IVF fully — a common partial answer just says 'the egg is fertilised outside the body'. AQA wants the hormonal stimulation step, the collection step, the fertilisation, and the implantation.

Mixing up FSH's role in contraception vs fertility treatment — in contraception, we SUPPRESS FSH (to prevent egg development). In IVF, we BOOST FSH (to stimulate multiple eggs). Same hormone, opposite use.
examTips: For menstrual cycle sequence questions, always cover all four hormones in order: FSH → oestrogen → LH surge → ovulation → progesterone → lining maintained (or breaks down if no fertilisation).

When explaining contraception, use the word 'inhibit' — 'oestrogen inhibits FSH production' — and then link it to the consequence: 'no FSH → no follicle matures → no ovulation → pregnancy prevented.' AQA mark schemes look for this chain.

For IVF questions, identify that FSH injections cause superovulation (production of multiple eggs), not just 'more hormones'. The word superovulation or 'stimulate multiple egg production' is worth using.
AQA sometimes asks about ethical issues with IVF — unused embryos, multiple births risk, access to treatment. These are valid evaluation points if the question asks you to 'discuss'.
revisionCardBullets: FSH (pituitary): stimulates follicle development and oestrogen production
Oestrogen (ovaries): thickens uterus lining; inhibits FSH; triggers LH surge when high
Progesterone (corpus luteum): maintains uterus lining; inhibits FSH and LH
No fertilisation → progesterone falls → lining breaks down → menstruation → new cycle
Contraceptive pill: synthetic oestrogen/progesterone inhibit FSH → no ovulation
IVF: FSH + LH injections → superovulation → eggs collected → fertilised in lab → embryo implanted
Progesterone may be given in IVF to maintain uterus lining for implantation
Negative feedback: oestrogen inhibits FSH; progesterone inhibits FSH + LH
Oestrogen at high levels switches to stimulating LH surge (exception to inhibition)
